old_claim.note: Socialism (Early Theories) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains from labor unions and collective bargaining to early socialist theories because the current labor-union node is anchored to 1799 British Combination Acts, while the socialism node is source-anchored to socialist terminology and movements around 1830.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica says socialism's origins as a political movement lie in the Industrial Revolution and that the term socialist came into use about 1830.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source anchors the early-socialist-theories node around 1830, after the current 1799 labor-union node date.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Labor combinations and collective bargaining could be shaped by industrial conditions and law before socialist terminology and utopian socialist movements became a direct intellectual predecessor.

ontology_before: The graph modeled 1799 labor unions as directly downstream of early socialist theories using an unsourced 1760s socialism date.
ontology_after: Labor unions remain downstream of factory systems, codified law, and printing, while early socialist theories remain as an intellectual predecessor for later Marxism and communism.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: labor_unions_collective_bargaining must not directly depend on socialism_early_theories under the current 1799 labor-union scope and 1830 socialism scope.
would_reject_if: The Labor Unions node is rescoped and redated to post-1830 explicitly socialist trade-union movements.
A separate pre-1799 proto-socialist doctrine node is introduced with source-backed chronology and a direct labor-union relationship.
A source shows the current 1799 British labor-union scope specifically depended on socialist theories rather than shared industrial conditions.
short_reason: The source-backed socialism node postdates the current labor-union node.
